summary: This chunk introduces the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), a fifteenth-century necromantic miscellany, and discusses its structure, content categories (illusionist, psychological, divinatory), and historical context. It includes a notable judicial record from 1437 involving Johannes Cunalis, a priest in Munich, who allegedly owned a book of necromancy; the demon Jubertus named—Luxuriosus, Superbus, Avarus—are explicitly listed. The manuscript contains rituals such as the Liber consecracionum and conjurations for spirits like Mirage, Lilith, and Semiforas.
key_passages: Interspersed with these are a version of the Liber consecracionum (no. 31); a list of spirits, with descriptions of the forms in which they appear and the functions they perform (no. 34);
The first two folios of the manuscript are missing, a circumstance which may help to explain how the manuscript evaded detection and thus survived.
One of the many subjects brought before the judges... was a sixty-year-old man, Jubertus of Bavaria... he served... Johannes Cunalis, who is a priest and plebanus, in a city called Munich...
...three demons, one named Luxuriosus, another Superbus, and the third Avarus, [all of them] devils. And the first appeared to him in the form of a charming maiden of twelve years...
The mirror of Floron, for revelation of past, present and future
